message.content: I have questions about 'time machine', the macos service for backing up.  I have two synology NAS devices, in different locations.  Can I have one mac backed up to both?

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Yes. macOS Time Machine supports multiple backup destinations and rotates between them automatically.

**Setup:**
1. On each Synology, enable the Time Machine shared folder (Control Panel → File Services → SMB/AFP, then create a shared folder with a user quota — Time Machine will fill whatever you give it).
2. On the Mac: System Settings → General → Time Machine → Add Backup Disk. Add both NAS destinations (connect via SMB to each one first so they mount).

**How rotation works:**
- Time Machine alternates between destinations on each hourly snapshot — not a simultaneous write. Each destination ends up with an independent, complete backup history.
- If one NAS is offline (e.g., the remote one over VPN), Time Machine just backs up to the available one and catches up later.
- Each destination has its own sparsebundle; they don't need to stay in sync.

**Practical notes for your two-location setup:**
- The remote NAS needs to be reachable when backups run. Options: site-to-site VPN, Tailscale (works well for this), or only back up to it when you're physically at that location.
- First backup to the remote NAS over WAN will be slow — consider seeding it locally first, then moving the NAS.
- Set a quota on each Synology share (~2-3× your Mac's used space) so Time Machine prunes old snapshots instead of filling the volume.
- SMB is the current recommendation; AFP is deprecated.

The main tradeoff: two destinations doubles your restore points and gives geographic redundancy, but a remote backup over a slow link can stall or fail partial backups — Tailscale or a reliable VPN makes this much smoother than raw port-forwarding.
